one_line_takeaway: By deliberately coaxing an open-source LLM to follow whatever instruction hides in a long context and then attention-attributing and deleting the tokens that drove that following, PISanitizer turns an attacker's own persuasiveness into the signal that gets them removed, cutting prompt-injection ASR to near zero at ~1.8s overhead while detection- and fine-tuning-based defenses fail on long contexts.
key_finding: PISanitizer drives attack success rate to near zero (typically 0.0-0.04) while restoring target-task utility to essentially the no-attack level across all six datasets and all seven backend LLMs, whereas the strongest prior defense Meta-SecAlign collapses under optimization attacks (GCG ASR near 1.0 on most datasets). On HotpotQA under Combined Attack, no-defense utility/ASR is 0.24/0.66 versus PISanitizer's 0.59/0.01; under GCG it is 0.12/0.96 versus 0.56/0.02. Token-level sanitization is accurate (avg 0.80 precision, 0.90 recall, 0.82 F1 under attack; only ~0.81 tokens removed on average from clean contexts). It is also efficient: ~1.8s to sanitize a several-thousand-token context, with total runtime (~12s) comparable to lightweight baselines and far below PromptLocate's ~633-761s. Critically, the paper demonstrates that state-of-the-art detection defenses do not transfer to long context (DataSentinel FPR=1.0 everywhere; PromptGuard FNR up to 0.99; AttentionTracker FPR=1.0), which undercuts detection+attribution pipelines. PISanitizer holds up under all six adaptive attacks (ASR <= 0.04), including repeated injections, sanitization-instruction-neutralizing prompts, token-spacing evasion, and a joint effectiveness+stealth nano-GCG attack.
core_contribution: PISanitizer is a prompt-sanitization defense against indirect prompt injection specifically for long-context LLMs. Rather than trying to make the backend LLM ignore injected instructions (the prevailing prevention approach) or first detecting whether a context is contaminated (detection/attribution approaches, which the paper shows fail on long contexts), PISanitizer inverts the logic: it prepends a 'sanitization instruction' that intentionally tells an LLM to follow ANY instruction in the given context, generates a single output token, and then treats the context tokens that receive the highest attention weight from that output token as the injected instruction and removes them. The key conceptual claim is a designed dilemma for the attacker: the more compellingly an injected instruction forces instruction-following, the higher its attention weight and the more reliably it is sanitized. The sanitized context is then handed to the (possibly closed-source, e.g., GPT-5) backend LLM for the real task.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection into a long target context (4K-20K tokens). A strong attacker has white-box access to the backend LLM's parameters and system prompt, knows the target instruction, can read the entire target context, and can inject a malicious instruction (plus a separator, together the 'injected prompt') at any position (beginning, middle, or end). The attacker's goal is to make the backend LLM follow the injected instruction and produce an attacker-desired output (e.g., a target answer, 'Hacked!', a sentiment label, or an arbitrary daily-task instruction). The defender has access to an LLM (which may differ from the backend LLM, e.g., the open-source Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) used to perform sanitization; the defender may be an app provider or an individual user. The paper explicitly evaluates adaptive attackers who know PISanitizer's mechanism and try to be both effective and stealthy (low attention under the sanitization instruction).
limitations: PISanitizer does not perfectly restore utility on removal-sensitive tasks, most visibly code generation (LCC): even after sanitization, utility stays below the clean baseline (e.g., 0.37 vs 0.42 clean on Llama-3.1-8B) because a few clean tokens are occasionally removed. The sanitization step fundamentally requires an open-source LLM whose per-layer/per-head attention weights are accessible, so a closed-source model cannot itself be the sanitizer (though it can still be the backend). The defense assumes injected instructions manifest as high-attention, spatially-contiguous token groups; the consecutive-token grouping heuristic (d=10) and threshold theta encode this and are the target of the evaluated evasion attempts. Threat model assumes indirect injection where the context is nominally pure 'data' with no legitimate instructions; tasks where the context legitimately contains instruction-like text could be harder. Evaluation subsamples 100/200 LongBench items per dataset, and adaptive attacks, while diverse, are the authors' own designs rather than an independent red team.

datasets_used: LongBench - Qasper (single-doc QA over NLP papers, F1)
LongBench - HotpotQA (multi-doc QA, F1; default target task)
LongBench - LCC (code completion, Edit Sim / Levenshtein)
quantitative_results: HotpotQA Combined Attack: no-defense Utility/ASR = 0.24/0.66 -> PISanitizer 0.59/0.01
Token-level sanitization under attack: avg precision 0.80, recall 0.90, F1 0.82; ~0.81 tokens removed on average from clean contexts
Efficiency: ~1.8s to sanitize a several-thousand-token context; total runtime ~10-14s vs PromptLocate ~633-761s
Meta-SecAlign under GCG: ASR ~1.0 on Qasper/HotpotQA/GovReport/PassageRetrieval (fails), vs PISanitizer ASR <=0.02
Detection baselines on long context (Combined Attack): DataSentinel FPR=1.0 (all 6 datasets), PromptGuard FNR up to 0.99, AttentionTracker FPR=1.0
Adaptive attacks (Table 10): PISanitizer ASR = 0.04/0.03/0.00/0.03/0.00/0.04 across Heuristic-I..IV, Optimization-I, Optimization-II (no-defense ASR 0.35-0.82)
Generalizes to GPT-5 backend: e.g., HotpotQA Combined Attack ASR 0.0 with utility 0.81 (clean 0.81)
baselines_compared: Meta-SecAlign (DPO fine-tuning prevention; prior SOTA, supersedes StruQ/SecAlign)
AttnTrace (attribution, combined with the above detectors)
